lessons.title: Imaginary Numbers and the Complex Plane
lessons.content:
### Why would anyone invent an "imaginary" number?

Start with a puzzle. What number, multiplied by itself, gives $-1$? With ordinary numbers there is no answer: a positive times a positive is positive, and a negative times a negative is also positive. So nothing squared can be negative. For a long time people simply said "no solution" and stopped.

Then mathematicians tried something bold: *invent* a brand-new number whose whole job is to square to $-1$, and see what happens. They called it the **imaginary unit**. Mathematicians write it $i$; engineers write it $j$ (because the letter $i$ is already used for electric current). They mean exactly the same thing. Its one rule is:
$$j^2 = -1.$$
That is the entire definition. "Imaginary" is just a historical name — it is no more fake than a negative number. It turned out to be incredibly useful, especially for describing waves and electricity.

### A number with two parts

Once you have $j$, you can build a **complex number**: a number with a *real* part and an *imaginary* part, written
$$z = a + jb.$$
Here $a$ and $b$ are ordinary real numbers. $a$ is the **real part** and $b$ is the **imaginary part**. For example $z = 3 + j2$ means "3, plus 2 of the imaginary unit." This form is called **rectangular form**.

The neat trick is that you can *draw* a complex number. Make a graph where the horizontal axis measures the real part and the vertical axis measures the imaginary part. This picture is the **complex plane**. To plot $z = 3 + j2$, go 3 right and 2 up, and mark a dot. ![a complex number plotted as a point](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoldCoin1987/Learning-App/main/packs/mathematics/images/complex-phasors-3.svg)

So a complex number is really just a point (or an arrow from the origin to that point) on a 2-D map. This same picture later describes the size and timing of an electrical wave.

### Recap

We invented $j$ so that $j^2 = -1$. A complex number $a + jb$ bundles a real part and an imaginary part into one object, and we picture it as a point on the complex plane: real to the right, imaginary up.

items.prompt: What is the defining rule of the imaginary unit?
choices: It is a real number a little bigger than 1
It cannot be multiplied at all
items.explanation: The imaginary unit was invented precisely to be a number whose square is $-1$ (written $j^2 = -1$). It is not a real number, since no real number squared is negative.

items.prompt: Engineers write $j$ instead of $i$ for the imaginary unit mainly because:
choices: the letter $i$ is already used for electric current
items.explanation: $i$ and $j$ mean the same thing. Engineers pick $j$ only to avoid clashing with $i$, the usual symbol for instantaneous current. The math is identical.

items.prompt: In the complex number $z = 5 + j7$, which is the imaginary part?
items.explanation: In $a + jb$, the imaginary part is the real number $b$ multiplying $j$ — here that is $7$. The real part is $5$. The imaginary part is a plain number, not $j7$ and not $j$ itself.

items.prompt: On the complex plane, what does the horizontal axis measure?
items.explanation: By convention the horizontal axis is the real axis (the real part) and the vertical axis is the imaginary axis (the imaginary part). That is what lets us plot $a + jb$ as a point.

items.prompt: Where would you place the point for $z = 3 + j2$?
choices: On the imaginary axis at height 5
items.explanation: The real part 3 moves you right along the real axis; the imaginary part 2 moves you up along the imaginary axis. Swapping them (option 3) would plot a different number, $2 + j3$.

items.prompt: A number like $z = 4$ (with no $j$ part) sits where on the complex plane?
choices: At the very top of the plane
items.explanation: Plain real numbers have imaginary part 0, so they lie on the real (horizontal) axis. Complex numbers simply *include* the ordinary real numbers as a special case.

items.prompt: Why is calling these numbers "imaginary" a bit misleading?
choices: Because they only exist inside computers
Because the name is historical; they are as real and useful as negative numbers
items.explanation: "Imaginary" is just an old, unfortunate name. These numbers obey clear rules, can be drawn on a plane, and describe real physical things like AC electricity and waves.

items.prompt: Which of these is written correctly in rectangular form $a + jb$?
items.explanation: Rectangular form is a real part plus $j$ times another real part: $a + jb$, e.g. $2 + j3$. The others are either a plain sum, a product, or a quotient — not the standard form.

items.prompt: Thinking of a complex number as an arrow from the origin, what are its two coordinates?
choices: Its real part (across) and imaginary part (up)
items.explanation: The arrow ends at the point $(a, b)$: the real part sets how far across, the imaginary part sets how far up. This two-coordinate picture is exactly why complex numbers can capture both a size and a direction.

lessons.title: Doing Arithmetic: Size, Angle, and the Conjugate
lessons.content:
### Working with two-part numbers

Now that a complex number is a point with a real and an imaginary part, how do we add, multiply, and measure them? The good news: it follows familiar rules, with one extra fact, $j^2 = -1$.

**Adding and subtracting** is easy — just combine the matching parts, like keeping dollars with dollars and cents with cents:
$$(2 + j3) + (1 - j5) = (2+1) + j(3-5) = 3 - j2.$$

**Multiplying** uses ordinary expansion (the "FOIL" method), and wherever $j^2$ appears you replace it with $-1$. For example $(a+jb)(c+jd) = ac + jad + jbc + j^2bd = (ac - bd) + j(ad + bc)$.

### Size and direction

Because a complex number is an arrow, it has a length and a direction. The **magnitude** (length of the arrow) is found with the Pythagorean theorem:
$$|z| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}.$$
So $z = 3 + j4$ has magnitude $\sqrt{9+16}=5$. Magnitude is never negative. The **argument** is the angle the arrow makes with the positive real axis, written $\theta$. Together, length $r=|z|$ and angle $\theta$ describe the same point as $a$ and $b$ do — just using "how long and which way" instead of "how far across and up." ![length r and angle theta of a complex number](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoldCoin1987/Learning-App/main/packs/mathematics/images/complex-phasors-4.svg)

### The conjugate: a handy mirror

The **conjugate** of $z = a + jb$ is $z^* = a - jb$: flip the sign of the imaginary part. On the plane this mirrors the point across the real axis. Its magic: multiplying a number by its own conjugate clears out $j$ and gives a plain real number,
$$z\,z^* = a^2 + b^2 = |z|^2.$$
That is exactly how we **divide**: to compute $1/(a+jb)$, multiply top and bottom by the conjugate $a - jb$, turning the bottom into the real number $a^2+b^2$.

### Recap

Add by parts, multiply with $j^2=-1$, measure length with $\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ and direction with the angle $\theta$, and use the conjugate (flip the imaginary sign) to make denominators real so you can divide.

items.explanation: Add the real parts ($2+1=3$) and the imaginary parts ($3 + (-5) = -2$) separately, giving $3 - j2$. You never mix a real part with an imaginary part when adding.

items.prompt: When multiplying complex numbers, what do you do every time a $j^2$ appears?
items.explanation: The defining rule $j^2 = -1$ is what makes complex multiplication work. Forgetting to substitute $-1$ (or using $+1$) is the most common beginner error.

items.prompt: What is the magnitude $|z|$ of $z = -3 + j4$?
items.explanation: $|z| = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9+16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$. The signs of $a$ and $b$ disappear because they are squared, so magnitude is always non-negative.

items.prompt: The conjugate of $z = a + jb$ is:
items.explanation: The conjugate flips only the sign of the imaginary part: $z^* = a - jb$. Geometrically this mirrors the point across the real axis.
A number times its conjugate equals $a^2 + b^2 = 3^2 + 4^2 = 25$, a real number with no $j$. This is exactly why conjugates are used to simplify division.

items.prompt: To divide by $a + jb$, the standard trick is to multiply the top and bottom by:
items.explanation: Multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate $a - jb$ turns the denominator into the real number $a^2 + b^2$, after which the result is easy to write in $a + jb$ form.

items.prompt: The 'argument' of a complex number refers to its:
choices: angle from the positive real axis
items.explanation: The argument is the direction of the arrow — the angle $\theta$ it makes with the positive real axis. The length is the separate quantity called the magnitude.

items.prompt: Why can the same point be described by either $(a, b)$ or by $(r, \theta)$?
choices: They are different points that happen to look alike
Because 'across and up' and 'length and direction' both pin down one location
items.explanation: Rectangular $(a,b)$ says how far across and up; polar $(r,\theta)$ says how long the arrow is and which way it points. Both specify the same single point — just two languages for it.

items.prompt: What is the magnitude of the real number $z = -6$ viewed as a complex number?
items.explanation: With $a = -6$ and $b = 0$, $|z| = \sqrt{(-6)^2 + 0^2} = 6$. Magnitude is a length, so it is the positive distance from the origin, never negative.

items.prompt: Multiplying a number by its conjugate is useful because the result is always:
items.explanation: $z\,z^* = a^2 + b^2 = |z|^2$, which has no imaginary part. Getting a clean real number is precisely what makes the conjugate the key tool for division and for computing magnitude.

lessons.title: Polar Form, Euler's Formula, and Roots of Unity
lessons.content:
### One number, two costumes

We saw a complex number can be described by its parts $a + jb$ (rectangular) or by its length and angle $r$ and $\theta$ (polar). Writing the polar version out, basic trigonometry gives the across-and-up pieces from the length and angle:
$$a = r\cos\theta,\qquad b = r\sin\theta.$$
Going back the other way, $r = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$ and $\theta$ is the angle of the arrow.

### Euler's formula: the bridge

Here is one of the most beautiful results in math, **Euler's formula**:
$$e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta.$$
In words: raising $e$ (about 2.718) to an imaginary power gives the point on a circle of radius 1 sitting at angle $\theta$. This lets us write any complex number compactly in **exponential form**:
$$z = r\,e^{j\theta}.$$
The length is $r$; the angle is $\theta$. A famous special case is $e^{j\pi} = -1$ (angle $\pi$ radians, i.e. $180^\circ$, points left to $-1$).

### Why exponential form is a power tool

Multiplying is suddenly trivial. If $z_1 = r_1 e^{j\theta_1}$ and $z_2 = r_2 e^{j\theta_2}$, then
$$z_1 z_2 = r_1 r_2\, e^{j(\theta_1 + \theta_2)}.$$
That is: **multiply the lengths, add the angles.** So multiplying by a complex number *scales and rotates*. Dividing subtracts the angles. Raising to a power $n$ scales the length to $r^n$ and multiplies the angle by $n$ — this is **De Moivre's theorem**: $(r e^{j\theta})^n = r^n e^{jn\theta}$.

### Roots of unity

Using this, we can ask: which numbers, raised to the power $N$, give $1$? These are the **$N$th roots of unity**. They are $N$ points spaced evenly around the unit circle, $360^\circ/N$ apart, starting at $1$. For $N = 8$ they step every $45^\circ$. ![the eight 8th roots of unity on the unit circle](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoldCoin1987/Learning-App/main/packs/mathematics/images/complex-phasors-2.svg) These evenly spaced points are the backbone of the Fourier transform used everywhere in audio, image, and radio processing — a nice hint of where this leads in engineering.

### Recap

Polar form uses length and angle; Euler's formula $e^{j\theta}=\cos\theta+j\sin\theta$ packs them into $r e^{j\theta}$. In that form, multiply = scale-and-rotate, powers follow De Moivre, and the roots of unity are evenly spaced dots on the unit circle.

items.prompt: State Euler's formula.
items.explanation: Euler's formula is $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$. The real part is the cosine and the imaginary part is the sine, placing the point on the unit circle at angle $\theta$.

items.prompt: In the exponential form $z = r\,e^{j\theta}$, what does $r$ represent?
items.explanation: In $r\,e^{j\theta}$, $r$ is the magnitude (length) and $\theta$ is the angle. This is just polar form written with the exponential, thanks to Euler's formula.

items.prompt: What is $e^{j\pi}$?
items.explanation: $e^{j\pi} = \cos\pi + j\sin\pi = -1 + j\cdot 0 = -1$. The angle $\pi$ (180 degrees) points straight left to the value $-1$ on the unit circle.

items.prompt: When you multiply two complex numbers in exponential form, you:
choices: add the lengths and multiply the angles
multiply the lengths and add the angles
multiply both the lengths and the angles
subtract the lengths and add the angles
items.explanation: $r_1 e^{j\theta_1} \cdot r_2 e^{j\theta_2} = r_1 r_2\, e^{j(\theta_1+\theta_2)}$: lengths multiply, angles add. This 'scale and rotate' rule is the whole reason exponential form is so convenient.

items.prompt: Multiply $z_1 = 2e^{j\pi/6}$ by $z_2 = 3e^{j\pi/3}$.
items.explanation: Multiply the lengths ($2\cdot 3 = 6$) and add the angles ($\pi/6 + \pi/3 = \pi/2$), giving $6e^{j\pi/2}$. Adding the lengths (option 1) is the common mistake.

items.prompt: Using De Moivre's idea, raising $r\,e^{j\theta}$ to the power $n$ gives:
items.explanation: $(r e^{j\theta})^n = r^n e^{jn\theta}$: the length is raised to the power $n$ and the angle is multiplied by $n$. This follows directly from 'multiply lengths, add angles' done $n$ times.

items.prompt: Convert $z = 1 + j$ to exponential form. (Hint: it sits at 45 degrees.)
items.explanation: The length is $r = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2}$ and the arrow points at $45^\circ = \pi/4$, so $z = \sqrt{2}\,e^{j\pi/4}$.

items.prompt: The $N$th roots of unity are best described as:
choices: $N$ points evenly spaced around the unit circle, starting at 1
points far outside the unit circle
items.explanation: Solving $z^N = 1$ gives $N$ points equally spaced on the unit circle, $360^\circ/N$ apart, beginning at $1$. They all have length 1, so they lie exactly on the circle.

items.prompt: How many distinct 4th roots of unity are there, and what are they?
items.explanation: There are 4 of them, spaced $90^\circ$ apart: $1, j, -1, -j$. Each one raised to the 4th power returns to $1$.

items.prompt: Geometrically, multiplying any complex number by $j$ does what?
items.explanation: Since $j = e^{j\pi/2}$ (length 1, angle $90^\circ$), multiplying by $j$ adds $90^\circ$ to the angle and leaves the length unchanged — a pure quarter-turn counterclockwise.

lessons.title: Phasors: Describing Waves with Complex Numbers
lessons.content:
### The problem phasors solve

Many things in the world wiggle smoothly up and down: the voltage in a wall socket, a sound tone, a radio signal. Such a smooth wave can be written
$$v(t) = V_m\cos(\omega t + \phi).$$
Don't be scared by the symbols. $V_m$ is the **size** of the wave (how tall its peaks are). $\omega$ ("omega") is how fast it wiggles. And $\phi$ ("phi") is the **timing** — whether this wave is a little ahead of or behind another one. When you combine several waves that wiggle at the same speed, doing it with the cosine formula and trig identities is painful. Phasors make it easy.

### A phasor: size and timing in one arrow

If every wave in a problem wiggles at the same speed $\omega$, then the only things that differ are the size $V_m$ and the timing $\phi$. A **phasor** is simply a complex number that stores exactly those two things — using the length for size and the angle for timing:
$$\mathbf{V} = V_m\,e^{j\phi}.$$
The common wiggle is left out because everyone shares it. So a phasor is "a compact way to describe a wave's size and timing as one arrow on the complex plane." ![a phasor arrow showing size and timing](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoldCoin1987/Learning-App/main/packs/mathematics/images/complex-phasors-5.svg) Adding two waves now just means adding two arrows — ordinary complex addition, no trig identities.

### Impedance: resistance for waves

In electricity, a **resistor** opposes current by a fixed amount $R$ (measured in ohms). But two other common parts, the **inductor** (a coil, symbol $L$) and the **capacitor** (symbol $C$), oppose *wiggling* current in a way that also shifts its timing. To capture both "how much" and "what timing shift," we use a complex number called **impedance**, written $Z$:
- Resistor: $Z_R = R$ (real — no timing shift).
- Inductor: $Z_L = j\omega L$ (the $j$ means a $90^\circ$ timing shift; bigger at higher wiggle speed).
- Capacitor: $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ (a $90^\circ$ shift the other way; smaller at higher speed).

The payoff: with phasors and impedance, the wave version of Ohm's law is just $\mathbf{V} = Z\,\mathbf{I}$ — the same simple multiply-and-divide you'd use for plain resistors. This is the everyday tool of electrical and radio engineering, and it all rests on the complex numbers from the earlier lessons.

### Recap

A phasor is a complex number holding a wave's size (length) and timing (angle), leaving out the shared wiggle. Impedance $Z$ extends this so that resistors, inductors, and capacitors all obey one tidy rule, $\mathbf{V} = Z\,\mathbf{I}$.

items.prompt: In a phasor, the two things a complex number stores about a wave are its:
items.explanation: A phasor uses the arrow's length for the wave's size (amplitude) and its angle for the timing (phase). The wiggle speed is left out because all waves in the problem share it.

items.prompt: In $v(t) = V_m\cos(\omega t + \phi)$, what does $V_m$ mean?
choices: the size of the wave (height of its peaks)
items.explanation: $V_m$ is the amplitude — how tall the wave's peaks are. The speed of wiggling is $\omega$, and the timing offset is $\phi$.

items.prompt: Why can we leave the common wiggle out of a phasor?
choices: because every wave in the problem shares the same wiggle speed, so only size and timing differ
items.explanation: When all signals share the same $\omega$, that part is identical for everyone and can be dropped. What is left to track is just the size and timing, which the phasor stores.

items.prompt: A phasor is written $\mathbf{V} = V_m\,e^{j\phi}$. Which form from earlier lessons does this match?
items.explanation: It is exactly the exponential form $r\,e^{j\theta}$, with the length $r$ being the size $V_m$ and the angle $\theta$ being the timing $\phi$. Phasors reuse the polar idea directly.

items.prompt: What is the main advantage of adding two waves as phasors instead of with trig?
choices: It turns the work into simple complex-number addition (adding arrows)
It removes the need for any numbers
items.explanation: Because phasors are just complex numbers, combining waves becomes ordinary complex addition — adding arrows on the plane — instead of wrestling with trigonometric identities.

items.prompt: What is impedance, in plain terms?
choices: a complex number describing how much a part opposes wiggling current and how it shifts the timing
the number of wires in a circuit
items.explanation: Impedance $Z$ generalizes resistance to waves: its size says how strongly the part opposes the current, and its angle (the imaginary part) captures the timing shift it introduces.

items.prompt: The impedance of an ideal resistor is:
items.explanation: A resistor's impedance is just $Z_R = R$, a real number. It opposes current but introduces no timing shift, so there is no imaginary part.

items.prompt: Which part has impedance $Z_L = j\omega L$, and what does the $j$ signal?
choices: a capacitor; the $j$ means no timing shift
an inductor; the $j$ means a 90-degree timing shift
a resistor; the $j$ is a typo
an inductor; the $j$ means the impedance is negative
items.explanation: $Z_L = j\omega L$ is the inductor. The factor $j$ (angle $90^\circ$) tells us the inductor shifts the timing between voltage and current by a quarter cycle.

items.prompt: With phasors and impedance, Ohm's law for waves takes the form:
items.explanation: It is $\mathbf{V} = Z\,\mathbf{I}$ — the same multiply/divide structure as the DC version $V = IR$, but with complex impedance $Z$ in place of plain resistance $R$.

items.prompt: An inductor's impedance is $Z_L = j\omega L$. What happens to it as the wiggle speed $\omega$ increases?
items.explanation: Since $Z_L = j\omega L$ grows with $\omega$, an inductor opposes faster-wiggling current more strongly. (A capacitor, $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$, does the opposite and opposes less at higher speeds.)

items.prompt: Why is the whole phasor-and-impedance method built on complex numbers?
choices: by accident; any numbers would do
because one complex number can carry both a size and a timing at once
because real numbers cannot be added
items.explanation: A wave needs two facts: how big and what timing. A complex number naturally holds two parts (length and angle), so it is the perfect container — which is exactly why all the earlier lessons lead here.
